questions.question: A service does `using var http = new HttpClient();` on every request and is called thousands of times per minute. What failure mode does this most directly cause?
options: Socket exhaustion — disposed sockets sit in `TIME_WAIT` until ephemeral ports run out and `SocketException` is thrown
An immediate `OutOfMemoryException` thrown from allocating far too many client objects on the managed heap
Stale DNS — the pinned connection keeps hitting a dead IP after a failover, scale, or blue-green event
Thread-pool starvation as each new client blocks an ASP.NET request thread until its response returns
questions.explanation: Each `new HttpClient()` opens its own connection pool; disposing it strands the underlying TCP socket in `TIME_WAIT` for ~4 minutes, so under load you exhaust ephemeral ports and get `SocketException`. Stale DNS is the symptom of the *opposite* mistake — one static client forever.

questions.question: Why is keeping one static `HttpClient` alive for the lifetime of the process *also* wrong, even though it fixes socket exhaustion?
options: It pins the connection so it never re-resolves DNS, and keeps hitting a dead IP after a failover or scale event
It is not thread-safe for concurrent requests, so parallel calls corrupt each other's headers and bodies
Static fields are garbage-collected unpredictably, so the client may be reclaimed mid-request and throw
It disables HTTP keep-alive entirely, forcing an expensive new TCP and TLS handshake on every single call
questions.explanation: A single long-lived client reuses sockets but pins the connection, so it never picks up DNS changes (failover, blue-green, scaling) and keeps hitting the dead IP. `HttpClient` *is* thread-safe for concurrent requests, so that distractor is false.

questions.question: How does `IHttpClientFactory` give you both socket reuse and DNS freshness at the same time?
options: It reuses the pooled `HttpMessageHandler` to share sockets, but rotates it on a schedule so a fresh handler re-resolves DNS
It opens a brand-new socket for every request but caches the DNS lookup result for two minutes between calls
It keeps a single handler alive forever and runs a dedicated background thread that refreshes DNS periodically
It disposes and recreates the `HttpClient` on every call while quietly keeping the underlying socket open
questions.explanation: The factory pools and reuses the underlying handler for socket efficiency, but rotates that handler (default 2 minutes via `SetHandlerLifetime`) so a fresh handler eventually re-resolves DNS. It does not open a socket per request.

questions.question: The default `HttpMessageHandler` lifetime used by `IHttpClientFactory` is 2 minutes, and it can be changed with `SetHandlerLifetime`.
questions.explanation: The default handler lifetime is 2 minutes, configurable via `SetHandlerLifetime` (e.g. `TimeSpan.FromMinutes(5)`). The handler lifetime — not the client lifetime — governs socket/DNS behavior.

questions.question: Which package is the successor to the deprecated `Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Polly`, and what does it wrap?
options: `Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience`, which wraps Polly v8
`Polly.Extensions.Http`, which still wraps Polly v7
`Microsoft.AspNetCore.Resilience`, wrapping a BCL handler
`System.Net.Http.Resilience`, with a custom retry engine
questions.explanation: `Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience` replaces the deprecated `Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Polly`; it wraps Polly v8 and plugs a resilience pipeline into the handler chain as a delegating handler via `AddStandardResilienceHandler()`.

questions.question: What is the correct outermost-to-innermost order of the five strategies in `AddStandardResilienceHandler()`?
options: Rate limiter → total timeout → retry → circuit breaker → per-attempt timeout
Total timeout → rate limiter → circuit breaker → retry → per-attempt timeout
Retry → rate limiter → total timeout → per-attempt timeout → circuit breaker
Circuit breaker → rate limiter → retry → total timeout → per-attempt timeout
questions.explanation: The standard pipeline chains, outermost to innermost: rate limiter, total timeout (covers all retries), retry, circuit breaker, then per-attempt timeout. The retry loop sits inside the total-timeout budget and outside the per-attempt timeout.

questions.question: Using `AddStandardResilienceHandler()` defaults — per-attempt timeout 10s, retry 3×, total timeout 30s — a dependency takes 12s on every call. What does the caller ultimately observe?
options: Each attempt trips the 10s per-attempt timeout and retries, but the 30s total timeout caps the call, which fails with a timeout
The very first call succeeds at 12s, because the per-attempt timeout only ever applies to the retry attempts that follow it
The call fails instantly with `BrokenCircuitException`, since the 12s response time already exceeds the 10s attempt limit
The call retries forever, because each per-attempt timeout resets the 30s total budget back to full every single time
questions.explanation: Each 12s attempt trips the 10s per-attempt timeout and is retried, but the 30s total timeout — sitting outside the retry — caps the whole operation, so after roughly the first few attempts the budget is exhausted and the call fails with a timeout. The total timeout is what stops infinite waiting.

questions.question: A `PaymentClient` POSTs a charge and is registered with a bare `AddStandardResilienceHandler()`. What is the risk and the correct fix?
options: The handler retries all HTTP methods by default, so a transient failure can double-charge — fix with `DisableForUnsafeHttpMethods()` and/or an idempotency key
POST is never retried by the standard handler under any default configuration, so there is genuinely no risk here and no change is needed
The real risk is socket exhaustion caused by retried POST bodies — fix it by raising the handler lifetime well above its two-minute default
The circuit breaker will start refusing every POST after the first failure — fix it by lowering the breaker's `FailureRatio` toward zero
questions.explanation: The standard handler retries *all* methods by default, so a transient failure on a POST can duplicate the write (double-charge). Disable retries for unsafe methods with `DisableForUnsafeHttpMethods()` and/or send an idempotency key so the gateway de-dupes.

questions.question: A teammate writes `Policy.Handle<HttpRequestException>().WaitAndRetryAsync(...)` and it won't compile against the current package. Why, and what is the v8 equivalent?
options: That fluent API is Polly v7, removed in v8 — use `new ResiliencePipelineBuilder().AddRetry(...).Build()` and `ExecuteAsync`
The method became async-only in the latest release, so adding `.ConfigureAwait(false)` to the chain is all it takes to make it compile
The `Policy` type simply moved namespaces in v8, so adding `using Polly.Legacy;` lets the old fluent chain compile and run unchanged
v8 renamed the method to `Policy.Handle<>().Retry(...)`, dropping the wait variant entirely, so you just remove the delay configuration
questions.explanation: Polly v8 removed v7's fluent `Policy.Handle<>().WaitAndRetry(...)` API. The current model builds a reusable, thread-safe `ResiliencePipeline` via `ResiliencePipelineBuilder` + strategy options + `Build()`, executed with `ExecuteAsync`.

questions.question: In a hand-built Polly v8 pipeline, which strategy executes outermost, given the order strategies are added to the builder?
options: Fallback — the first strategy added is outermost and wraps the rest, so it catches whatever escapes the inner strategies
Timeout — the last strategy added always runs first, becoming the outermost guard around all the others in the chain
Circuit breaker — breakers are special and are always promoted to run before any other strategy, regardless of add order
Retry — the builder always lifts the retry strategy to the outermost position so it can re-issue the whole pipeline
questions.explanation: The first strategy added is outermost; strategies nest like middleware. Here fallback wraps retry, which wraps the circuit breaker, which wraps the timeout — so the per-attempt timeout is what retry re-issues and fallback catches whatever escapes.

questions.question: When a Polly v8 timeout strategy fires, which exception is thrown — and which `ShouldHandle` predicate must catch it?
options: `TimeoutRejectedException` — not the BCL `TimeoutException`, so predicates must handle that specific type
`TimeoutException` from the BCL, so handling `System.TimeoutException` in the predicate is the correct choice
`OperationCanceledException`, because the timeout strategy simply cancels the underlying cancellation token
`BrokenCircuitException`, since Polly internally treats an elapsed timeout as a circuit-breaker trip event
questions.explanation: Polly's timeout strategy throws `TimeoutRejectedException`, not the BCL `TimeoutException`. A `ShouldHandle` predicate that expects `System.TimeoutException` will silently miss it.

questions.question: Setting `UseJitter = true` on a retry strategy randomizes each backoff delay so that many clients don't all retry on the same tick, preventing a synchronized retry storm.
questions.explanation: Exponential backoff spaces attempts apart, but without jitter thousands of clients retry on the same tick (thundering herd). `UseJitter = true` desynchronizes them, which is the single toggle that breaks up the retry storm.

questions.question: Which statements about Polly v8's circuit breaker trigger and behavior are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: It opens only when the failure *ratio* exceeds the threshold AND at least `MinimumThroughput` calls occurred within the sampling window
While Open, calls throw `BrokenCircuitException` immediately with no network round-trip
After `BreakDuration` elapses it goes Half-Open and lets one trial call decide whether to close or re-open
It opens as soon as any single call fails, regardless of call volume
While Open it keeps issuing calls but logs each failure until `BreakDuration` elapses
questions.explanation: The v8 breaker uses a sampling window: it opens only when the failure ratio is exceeded *and* `MinimumThroughput` was met, then fails fast with `BrokenCircuitException` while Open, and probes once in Half-Open after `BreakDuration`. It does not trip on a single failure, nor does it keep calling while Open.

questions.question: Where in a resilience pipeline should the fallback strategy sit, and why?
options: Outermost, so it catches everything the inner strategies surface and can substitute a safe default value
Innermost, so it runs right before the network call and can short-circuit the request as early as possible
Right after retry but before the circuit breaker, so it only ever catches the case of retry attempts being exhausted
Nowhere alongside a circuit breaker — the two strategies both handle failures and must never be combined in one pipeline
questions.explanation: Fallback belongs outermost so it catches everything the inner strategies surface (including `BrokenCircuitException`) and supplies a substitute outcome — cached data, a default object — letting the caller degrade gracefully. It composes fine with a breaker.

questions.question: Your team wants to add custom retry behavior on top of `AddStandardResilienceHandler()` for one client. What is the correct guidance?
options: Add exactly one resilience handler per client; for a custom composition use `AddResilienceHandler("name", ...)` and build the pipeline yourself
Call `AddStandardResilienceHandler()` a second time on the same client — the later call simply merges its options into the first one
Stack a second `AddStandardResilienceHandler()` and lower that one's retry count so the two standard pipelines cooperate cleanly
Wrap the client inside another typed client that also adds a standard handler, deliberately layering the two pipelines together
questions.explanation: The rule is exactly one resilience handler per client — stacking handlers multiplies retries and timeouts unpredictably. For a custom composition, drop the standard handler and use `AddResilienceHandler("name", builder => ...)` to build the pipeline yourself.

questions.question: Stashing the `HttpClient` injected into a typed client in a static field or a singleton is safe because the factory already pools the handler behind it.
questions.explanation: Capturing the injected `HttpClient` statically or in a singleton re-pins the handler and defeats the rotation that keeps DNS fresh. The typed client is transient *precisely* so each use gets a fresh client over a rotating pooled handler — let the factory hand you a new one.

questions.question: Which statements about using Polly v8 resilience pipelines for non-HTTP work and for testing are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: A `ResiliencePipeline` wraps any delegate, so the same retry/breaker patterns apply to DB calls, queue publishes, and gRPC
Shared named pipelines can be registered with `AddResiliencePipeline` and resolved via `ResiliencePipelineProvider<TKey>` so they're built once
`ResiliencePipeline.Empty` is a no-op pipeline useful for bypassing resilience in tests
Resilience pipelines only function inside the HTTP handler chain and cannot wrap a `SaveChangesAsync` call
To test retry behavior you must make real network calls; in-memory `DelegatingHandler` stubs cannot drive the retry path
questions.explanation: A `ResiliencePipeline` is not HTTP-specific — it wraps any delegate (DB, queue, gRPC), shared pipelines register via `AddResiliencePipeline` and resolve through `ResiliencePipelineProvider<TKey>`, and `ResiliencePipeline.Empty` is the no-op for tests. You can absolutely unit-test the strategy with a `DelegatingHandler` stub that returns 503 a fixed number of times, no real network needed.

questions.question: Your microservice's typed client retries 3×, and the service mesh (Envoy/Istio) in front of the dependency *also* retries. What is the senior concern and the correct response?
options: Retries multiply across layers (client × mesh), amplifying load on a struggling dependency — pick one layer to own retries
The two retry layers automatically deduplicate their attempts, so the effective count stays 3 and no configuration change is needed
The mesh retry silently overrides the client retry, so the client's own retry config is ignored entirely and can be left exactly as-is
Client retries always take precedence over mesh retries, so the right move is to disable the mesh and quadruple the client retry count
questions.explanation: If both the client and the mesh/gateway retry, attempts multiply (e.g. 3×3), turning one slow service into a system-wide brownout. The fix is to pick a single layer to own retries rather than stacking them — there is no automatic dedup or override.
